metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Scenario

Scenario-meanings `shell-escape-command`: the agent runs a shell command outside the formal tool framework; irrlicht stays `working` during execution and resolves to `ready` when the command exits. User-observable signal: `ready → working → ready`, no stick in `working`.

## agent_supports: yes

Codex 0.130.0's TUI has a user-typed shell escape. The binary string is literal: "Prefix a command with `!` to run it locally. Example: `!ls`" (strings scan of the darwin-arm64 vendor binary). Typed empirically in a tmux probe: `!ls` renders "• You ran ls" and `!sleep 5` renders "• Running sleep 5" then "• Working (1s • esc to interrupt)" for the full 5 s, then "• You ran sleep 5". So the TUI itself holds the session in a working/interruptible state for the entire command runtime and returns to the prompt when it exits — the exact `ready → working → ready` shape the scenario describes, with no LLM round-trip.

This is a genuinely distinct affordance from `full-lifecycle-toolcall`: that scenario exercises the model's `exec`/`function_call` tool (assistant decides to run a command); this one is a USER-initiated `!`-prefix shell escape with no model turn. The rollout proves the difference — a `!`-escape writes NO `function_call`/`function_call_output`, only a `task_started`, a synthetic `user_message` (role=user) whose content is a `<user_shell_command><command>…</command><result>Exit code: 0…</result></user_shell_command>` block, and a `task_complete`.

## daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

The daemon (FilesUnderRoot watch on `~/.codex/sessions`, codex parser) DOES observe a `ready → working → ready` arc for a `!`-escape, with no stuck-working and no premature-ready — the load-bearing intent of the scenario. Confirmed against the live recording daemon (pid 98544) for the probe session: two clean `state_transition` pairs (ready→working `force ready→working on first activity`, working→ready `agent finished turn → ready`), one per `!`-escape, and a terminal `process_exited`.

The gap that makes this `partial` rather than `yes`: the working DWELL the daemon records does NOT cover the command's actual runtime. Codex writes `task_started` to the rollout the instant the command begins, but the codex parser skips `task_started` (parser.go:155-172 emits only `task_complete` as `turn_done`); the only activity the parser turns into a state change is the synthetic `user_message`, which codex does not flush until the command FINISHES. For `!sleep 5` the rollout showed `task_started` at T+0, then `user_message`+`task_complete` together at T+5s. The daemon therefore transitioned ready→working only at command-completion time and settled back to ready ~2 s later (its debounce window), so the recorded working arc is debounce-bounded (~2 s) rather than command-bounded (~5 s). Topology and final state are correct and observable; the literal "stays working FOR THE DURATION" is not, because the parser has no event to anchor the working start to until the command is already done.

## Capability anchor

Primitive exercised = shell-escape mechanism (Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous). Codex's analogue is the TUI `!`-prefix, anchored to `capabilities.json -> features.tui: true` (the escape is a TUI-only affordance; codex `exec` headless has no interactive `!` shell mode).
caveats: Working dwell is debounce-bounded (~2 s), not command-bounded: the codex parser skips task_started, and codex only flushes the synthetic user_message when the command exits, so the daemon cannot anchor the working-start to the command-start. Topology (ready→working→ready) and final state are correct; the literal 'stays working for the whole command' is not directly observable. This is the feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant pattern applied to a timing detail.
The !-escape is TUI-only; codex exec (headless) has no interactive ! shell mode. The scenario's requires:[headless_mode] is a scenario-level field; this cell is recorded interactively via tmux.

Unlike claudecode (whose parser deliberately skips <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> so state stays ready throughout), codex's !-escape DOES synthesize a turn (task_started/user_message/task_complete) and DOES drive a ready→working→ready arc.

sources.note: event_msg/task_complete → turn_done; task_started and other event_msg payloads skipped (lines 155-172); user/developer message → user_message with ClearToolNames
rule 4 default 'transcript activity → working'; rule 2b 'agent finished turn → ready' on turn_done — the two transitions observed for the probe
features.tui: true — the capability anchor for the !-prefix shell escape
strings scan: 'Prefix a command with ! to run it locally. Example: !ls'
!ls => task_started/user_message(<user_shell_command>)/task_complete same-ms; !sleep 5 => task_started at T+0, user_message+task_complete at T+5s, no function_call

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (codex 0.130.0; the !-prefix shell escape is a TUI affordance, confirmed in-binary: 'Prefix a command with ! to run it locally. Example: !ls')
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
codex is launched in interactive TUI mode (--no-alt-screen) so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — codex exec headless has no interactive ! shell mode
setup: Driver boots a codex TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits out the Booting MCP phase
Codex assigns its own rollout UUID; the driver discovers the rollout-*.jsonl on the first wait_turn
recipe.recipe_notes: Each !cmd escape is typed as a keys step WITHOUT Enter, then a 1s sleep, then a separate keys:'Enter' step. The codex driver's keys handler (unlike step_send) does not pause before Enter, so a combined '! l s Enter' raced codex's Ink input render — Enter was silently dropped, the escapes accumulated in the input box, and the final send submitted ls/sleep/prompt as ONE shell command (collapsed into a single <user_shell_command>). Splitting typing from Enter with a render-settle sleep submits each escape on its own. The 9s sleep after !sleep 5 covers the command runtime plus the daemon settle.

script.keys: ! s l e e p Space 5

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the codex rollout session entering ready after launch

each !cmd shell escape drives a ready→working→ready arc for the session (codex synthesizes a turn: task_started / user_message<user_shell_command> / task_complete) — UNLIKE claudecode, which skips the wrappers and stays ready
no state_transition gets stuck in working: every working transition is followed by a working→ready before the next escape or the final prompt
after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the session, proving it is still alive

NOTE (overlap + caveat): this is a USER !-escape, distinct from full-lifecycle-toolcall's model-driven exec tool (no function_call/function_call_output is written for a !-escape). The recorded working DWELL is debounce-bounded (~2 s), not command-bounded: codex's parser skips task_started and codex flushes the synthetic user_message only when the command exits, so the daemon cannot anchor working-start to command-start (see assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial). The spec therefore asserts the ready→working→ready topology and final ready, not a dwell length.
